data: I Taccuini. I. Riproduzione anastatica e trascrizione dei Taccuini 1-4

Paolo Orsi, I Taccuini. I. Riproduzione anastatica e trascrizione dei Taccuini 1-4, a c. di Gioconda Lamagna e Giuseppina Monterosso, Accademia Nazionale dei Lincei. Monumenti Antichi. Serie Miscellanea 20 (Rome: Giorgio Bretschneider Editore, 2018)
Fragment of a monumental Latin inscription
A single line of monumental lettering preserved, with a vacat below to the moulding.
Deep v-cut letters of high monumental quality with shallow serifs and a reasonably wide module.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2019-10-01
Orsi, 2018, I Taccuini. I. Riproduzione anastatica e trascrizione dei Taccuini 1-4
fragment of a monumental latin inscription
Each text is incised on a single line, in the first case on the right as one enters, in the other three cases on the left as one enters
4th — 3rd century BCE, but whether part of a Dionysian, Agathoclean, or Hieronian phase unclear.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana
Beste and Mertens, 2015, Die Mauern von Syrakus. Das Kastell Euryalos und die Befestigung der Epipolai
The text includes significant white space, implying a monumental public text
Manganaro, 1989, Iscrizioni Latine nuove e vecchie della Sicilia
Orsi, 1876-, Notizie degli scavi di antichità
Ephemeris epigraphica. Corporis inscriptionum Latinarum supplementum, edita iussu Instituti archaeologici Romani, 1872-1913
Libertini, 1929, Il regio museo archeologico di Siracusa
Remains of two lines of monumental text.
Monumental v-cut letters with modest serifs (line 1 is incomplete, but greater than 60mm).
Photo J.Prag (2018-09-15), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Tall thin letters, with thin incision, and extended serfis. T is substantially taller than other letters.
Roman, perhaps second or third century CE?
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2015-03-27
Fragmentary monumental Latin inscription from the amphitheatre
Area archeologica della Neapolis, Orecchio di Dionisio e Teatro Greco
Wilson, 1980, On the date of the Roman amphitheatre at Syracuse
Manganaro, 1988, La Sicilia da Sesto Pompeo a Diocleziano
Wilson, 1990, Sicily under the Roman Empire: the archaeology of a Roman province, 36 B.C. - A.D. 535
fragmentary monumental latin inscription from the amphitheatre
area archeologica della neapolis, orecchio di dionisio e teatro greco
The text is engraved in a single line across the middle of the three vertical surfaces on the front face of the stone.

Tall, narrow and slightly uneven, v-cut letters with some use of serifs; diminishing in height towards line end. A has a cross-bar which extends beyond the uprights on both sides, sloping down to the left; E has strokes of approximately equal length; P and R are open; S has larger upper half.
The formula perpetuus Augustus is typical of the fourth century
Photo J. Prag (2018), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
The Technitai of Dionysos honour Skymnos

Neat and regular letters of the later Hellenistic period, in some cases with pronounced serifs. Omicron is full-size; omega is closed; epsilon has shorter middle bar; mu has splayed outer hastae; kappa on occasion has short arms; sigma has parallel top and bottom strokes; pi has equal vertical strokes and the horizontal overhangs on both sides.
Photo J. Prag, Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Kaibel, 1890, Inscriptiones Graecae Siciliae et Italiae, additis graecis Galliae Hispaniae, Britanniae, Germaniae inscriptionibus
Manganaro, 1963, Nuove ricerche di epigrafia siceliota
Rizzo, 1923, Il teatro greco di Siracusa
Le Guen, 2001, Les associations de Technites dionysiaques à l'époque hellénistique
Aneziri, 2003, Die Vereine der dionysischen Techniten im Kontext der hellenistischen Gesellschaft: Untersuchungen zur Geschichte, Organisation und Wirkung der hellenistischen Technitenvereine
the technitai of dionysos honour skymnos
The Technitai of Dionysos honour Apollodotos son of Leukios

The letters are notably less regular than . Omicron is full; omega is closed; mu has splayed hastae and extended upper strokes; sigma has parallel top and bottom strokes; psi has short upward angled arms; epsilon has nearly equal length bars
the technitai of dionysos honour apollodotos son of leukios
Antonio Ferrua, «Epigrafica sicula pagana e cristiana», Rivista di Archeologia Cristiana 18 (1941): 151–243
Five lines, with left and right justification, vacat below.
Lunate epsilon and sigma, mu tending to cursive.
Strazzulla, 1897, Museum epigraphicum: seu inscriptionum Christianarum quae in Syracusanis catacumbis repertae sunt corpusculum
Ferrua, 1941, Epigrafica sicula pagana e cristiana
Text encoding: Valentina Mignosa; edition source: http://zotero.org/groups/382445/items/4HR4GXBX
Four lines, centred on the stone, diminishing in size from top to bottom.

I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo Museo archeologico regionale Paolo Orsi di Siracusa
Text encoding: Jonathan Prag; edition source: image
Siracusa. Esplorazioni nelle catacombe di S. Giovanni ed in quelle della vigna Cassia
Paolo Orsi, «Siracusa. Esplorazioni nelle catacombe di S. Giovanni ed in quelle della vigna Cassia», Notizie degli Scavi di Antichità, 1893, 276–314
lunate epsilon and sigma, uncial omega
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2015-10-21
Orsi, 1893, Siracusa. Esplorazioni nelle catacombe di S. Giovanni ed in quelle della vigna Cassia
Text encoding: Valentina Mignosa, Jonathan Prag; edition source: http://zotero.org/groups/382445/items/V7HNHTAG

The text appears to observes the shape of the stone, with a fixed left margin and the lines fitting to the line of breakage on the right, and a staurogram centrally filling the space below. The letters retain traces of red paint throughout.

Slightly irregular but neatly carved plain letters without serifs. Alpha has straight bar; Epsilon is rectilinear, with mostly equal length bars; Theta is large and voice, with a full width horizontal bar; Kappa generally has short arms; Lamda has a slight extension at the top; Mu (in ligature) has vertical virst and last, with diagonals extending only half-way down;' rho has small head, closed; sigma is lunate but rectilinear; omega is lunate. Interpuncts are used around numerals.
Photo Museo Arch. Reg. P. Orsi (2012), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Text encoding: Valentina Mignosa, Jonathan Prag
410 CE (dated to the year following the consulship of Honorius and Theodosius (409))
Agnello, 1953, Silloge di iscrizioni paleocristiane della Sicilia
Ferrua, 1982-1983, Le iscrizioni datate della Sicilia paleocristiana
Manganaro, 1993, Greco nei pagi e latino nelle città della Sicilia romana tra I e VI sec. d.C.
3rd — 5th century CE; purchase notice of locus in catacomb (?)
Wessel, 1989, Inscriptiones Graecae Christianae Veteres Occidentis
Text on two lines, with final letter of line 1 squeezed in small before the broken edge of the stone; two birds and christian monograms fill the space below.

Moderately neat and regular letters, cut with a broad groove rather than v-cut, without scerifs, but with extensions to tops of some letters (alpha, delta). alpha has broken bar; epsilon is lunate; kappa has full length arms; omicron is full size; rho has small almost closed eye; sigma is lunate but rectilinear; phi is circular with extended vertical; omega (in symbols below) is lunate.
Carini, 1872, Annotazioni sul sarcofago rinvenuto in Siracusa
Funerary inscription for Eirene and Theodoulos
hederae at the four corners, and two christograms to the right of the final line in a circle, with a cross between them and below.
Crispi, 1823, Iscrizioni sepolcrali greche appartenenti all'epoca cristiana, interpetrate ed illustrate
Ferrua, 1989, Note e giunte alle iscrizioni cristiane antiche della Sicilia
Muscolino, 2011-2012, Epigrafi funerarie greche e latine di Taormina
funerary inscription for eirene and theodoulos
over five lines, consistent left margin.
traces of red paint noted in the inventory

The text maintains a regular left margin, ignoring the irregularity of the form of the stone, and filling the space to the right; however, the cutter ran out of space at the end of the stone, and inserted the remaining letters of the word Δεκεμβρίῳ and the concluding staurogram, firstly at the end of line 2 (letter β) and then, under a 'paragraphos' line to the left of the main text of line 2, level with lines 3 (letters ριω) and 4 (staurogram)

plainly cut, relatively regular letters, in a curved groove rather than v-cut, without serifs. Alpha has an oblique crossbar, sloping down to left, and a horizontal stroke to left off the top of the letter; beta has closed eyes, the upper is smaller; epsilon is lunate (except for the first in line 5 which is rectilinear); zeta has regular Z form; kappa has near full-legnth arms; mu has cursive form; omicron is full size; rho has closed eye; sigma is lunate; omega is lunate.
Photo Museo Arch. Reg. P. Orsi (2015), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Feissel, 1982, Remarques de toponymie syrienne d'après des inscriptions grecques chrétiennes trouvées hors de Syrie
Five lines of Greek letters following the left margin of the stone.
Somewhat irregular lunate letters, tending strongly to cursive, especially the eta, alpha, mu and xsi
later Roman, but not certain whether Christian or Pagan (Ferrua)
Text encoding: James Hua, Jonathan Prag

Seven lines of Greek letters, with traces of red paint. Lines 1-3 are more formally / monumentally carved, from line four onwards the carving is lighter and less carefully laid out, rising to the right.

Simple, thinly incised letters without serfis or terminations. The letters become increasingly ireregular and variable in the final lines. Alpha has striaght bar but also broken and oblique bar; beta has closed roughly equal eyes; Epsilon is quadrate with equal length bars; Zeta is Z in form but irregular; theta is ovoid with full straight bar; kappa has shorter arms; omicron mostly slightly smaller than other letters; sigma is lunate but square in form, lower bar slanting downwards, except for the irregular curved instance end of line 2; omega is in the form of a w, but formed entirely of straight lines and right angles. interlineation is highly irregular throughout.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo I. Gradante, 2022-06-21
Six lines of text, of which the first four are neatly and regularly set out between guidelines, but the final two are squeezed into the remaining space, centred but unevenly and without guidelines.

Clear V-cut letters without serifs of somewhat uneven width, consistent height over the first four lines, more irregular thereafter. Alpha with broken bar and stroke rising off the apex; Beta with closed but separated eyes; delta with stroke rising off the apex; epsilon lunate in line 1 but regular in line 2; theta ovoid with full straight cross-bar; kappa has full length arms; mu has vertical first and last, and short middle strokes; omicron varies in size and from round to ovoid; pi is equal length with overlapping horizontal; rho has small closed eye; sigma is lunate, but rectilinear; omega is lunate and small above the line.
L'année épigraphique: revue des publications épigraphiques relatives a l'antiquité romaine., 1888-
Clarke, 2014, Il fascino della Cava. La storia dei Viaggiatori Inglesi a Cava Ispica
Rizzone, 2016, Una sinagoga a Cava Ispica
Griesheimer, 1989, Quelques inscriptions chrétiennes de Sicile orientale
Two texts, the second incised more lightly below the first.
Ferrua, 1938-1939, Note di epigrafia cristiana siracusana
The material from cava d'Ispica is bounded by two dated texts, of 398 CE and 468 CE, and these provide approximate lower and upper limits to the likely range (so Paci)
Bergk, 1843, Inschriften aus den Sicilischen Cyclopenhoehlen
Photo Museo Archeologico Regionale Paolo Orsi, neg. 19982 (1965); Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Orsi, 1890, Megara Hyblaea. Storia - topografia - necropoli e anathemata
Manni Piraino, 1975, Koiné alfabetica fra Siracusa, Megara Iblea e Selinunte?
Jeffery,Johnston, 1990, The local scripts of archaic Greece
Jeffery, 1961, The Local Scripts of Archaic Greece
Remains of two lines of Latin letters, damaged at both ends; whether more missing from above and below is unknown
The L in line two appears tall and narrow with a downward sloping second stroke, more typical of later Roman texts
Well-spaced lines filling the width of the available stone, word-breaks do not observe line-end.

Somewhat irregular deep-cut letters with pronounced terminations and occasionally extravagant serifs/flourishes (e.g. on X or E), and a varying tendency to the later narrow elongated style (e.g. TFL in line 5), and M sometimes with the upper strokes extended well above the join. A consistently lacks a cross-bar; E generally has equal length strokes, but with the top stroke often extended upwards; F, tall, narrow, and upward pointing; M sometimes with extended apices, sometimes without (all four strokes full length); P is closed; R sometimes open, with extended tail; X frequently over extended upper right. No interpuncts.
mid-2nd century to mid-4th century CE
Photo J. Prag (2015), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Thinly incised letters; line 5 is smaller than the rest.
Fragment of a monumental Latin text
Remains of two monumental letters, no original margin preserved; possible trace of the top of a letter on the lower edge?
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo I. Gradante 2022-02-15
Ferrua, 1946-1947, Florilegio d'iscrizioni paleocristiane di Sicilia
fragment of a monumental latin text
Text encoding: Jonathan Prag; edition source: #IG
Text over 5 lines, reducing drastically in size towards the lower edge.
lunate eipsilon and sigma, uncial omega, use of ligatures and supralines, and some interpunctuation.
Remains of four lines of Greek text, of which the preserved traces suggest that lines 1-3 filled the width of the stone, but line 4 is more widely spaced and has a vacat at the right, and below.

Regular widely spaced, finely incised letters, of consistent height throughout. Letters have basic triangular terminations to the strokes. Alpha is broken-bar; epsilon has short middle stroke (whether detached or not is unclear); kappa has equal length short arms; sigma has parallel top and bottom strokes, with the middle strokes extending half-width.
The letter forms would suggest later third or more likely second century BCE
Manganaro, 1961, Ricerche di epigrafia siceliota. I. Per la storia del culto delle divinita orientali in Sicilia
Sfameni Gasparro, 1973, I culti orientali in Sicilia
Vidman, 1969, Sylloge inscriptionum religionis Isiacae et Sarapiacae
Four lines of Greek letters; the text appears to be centred on the stone, with slight vacat below.

. Based upon the single published photograph: regularly and neatly carved fine lettters without serfis or significant terminations. Alpha has straight bar; Mu has vertical first and last, with midstrokes descending halfway; omicron is small and mid-line; sigma has diverging top and bottom strokes, the mid-strokes meeting at half width; omega is nearly full height, slightly ovoid, open with short horizontal tails. No editor records the size of the letters.
4th century or 3rd century BCE based on the letters.
Cavallari, 1876-, Notizie degli scavi di antichità
The text is set out within a tabula ansata, 26 cm x 18 cm, and heavily eroded, over four lines
Lunate sigma and epsilon, but little detail is visible.
Photo J. Prag (2013), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Manganaro, 1962, Graffiti e iscrizioni funerarie della Sicilia orientale
Sirena, 2011, Via Pompeia. L'antico tracciato stradale tra Messina e Siracusa
Bommara and Rizzone, 2007, Contributo alla conoscenza del territorio siracusano: recenti indagini a Priolo Gargallo
Rizzone, 2014-2015, La documentazione epigrafica tardoantica del territorio di Priolo Gargallo: una messa a punto

(Following Orsi's apograph in his taccuino) Small regular neat letters with terminations to the strokes but no signficant serifs. Alpha has straight bar; Epislon has slightly shortened middle bar; Zeta is vertical with wide top and bottom horizontal strokes; Kappa has slightly shortened arms; Mu has vertical first and last, with the middle strokes almost reaching to the base line; omicron is full-size; Sigma has parallel top and bottom strokes; Omega is round full-sized, and almost closed.
2nd or 1st century BCE by the lettering which is later Hellenistic
Manganaro, 1999, Sikelika: studi di antichità e di epigrafia della Sicilia greca
Fragment of a monumental Greek inscription
Remains of two, possibly three lines in large regular characters
Described by Orsi as large, beautiful, geometric characters. Omicron appears full size; pi has verticals of equal length; sigma four bar with horizontal top and bottom strokes.
later Hellenistic in form, but no precise date possible.
fragment of a monumental greek inscription
A single line of monumental letters across the broad face of the block
Elegant deep (14mm), v-cut letters of regular form. Kappa with short equal arms; sigma with horizontal top and bottom strokes; alpha with straight bar.
Rizzone, 2008, Iscrizioni tardoantiche del territorio di Scicli
Three lines of large relatively thin letters are partially preserved.
Tall, narrow letters, v-cut, with clear terminations/serifs at stroke ends. E is narrow with strokes of equal length; M has full-length strokes, first and last near vertical.
Photo J. Prag (2014), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Remains of three lines of text, of which only the middle line is preserved to full height. Text is laid out between double guidelines.

Relatively tall and thin letters, but still of regular square form, of somewhat irregular width, with short basic serifs at the end of strokes; somewhat roughly chiselled. R has an extended tail which does not reach to the lower line; O is tall and narrow; E has slightly shorter middle stroke. M is narrow, with very short middle strokes that join at the midpoint and join  the verticals well below the top.

Two lines of Greek text, with a deep horizontal line above each (same depth as the letters). Although the stone is broken top and right, there is no clear evidence that the text continued beyond what is preserved (the surface is preserved beyond the text, and the 'guidelines' also stop with the preserved text. A cross precedes line 1.

Deeply and neatly cut letters (given the material). Xsi formed by multiple straight strokes, alternately horizontal and diagonal; epsilon has equal length bars; Nu has the diagonal not reaching to either top or bottom of the vertical strokes; beta is closed, with lower eye larger than upper; alpha has an extended upper serif, and crossbar is oblique, descending to left; sigma is lunate; kappa has full length arms.
A single line of crudely incised Latin letters, with two faintly incised horizontal lines running across the face some distance below the text.
Curdely cut letters with basic serifs on some strokes; the mid-strokes of the M only descen halfway.
later Roman, after the second century BCE
